steps.title: Identify the temporal clause structure
steps.content: The key signal is the temporal conjunction “until” following a simple present main clause. The sentence reads: “Atoms...travel faster and faster until they ______ a desired energy level.” This structure requires a specific verb form in the subordinate clause.

steps.title: Apply the temporal clause rule
steps.content:
Subordinate temporal clauses (introduced by until, before, when, after, etc.) use simple present tense for future conditions, not future or past tense. “Reach” (B) is simple present and correctly expresses the condition the atoms must meet.

The other options are wrong: “will reach” (A) uses future tense incorrectly, “had reached” (C) uses past perfect, and “are reaching” (D) uses present progressive—none of which work for a temporal subordinate clause.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
